nodes.name: Calibration Curve Construction
nodes.description: A calibration curve relates instrument signal to concentration: measure a standard series, regress signal on concentration, reject outliers or re-prepare if the fit is poor, confirm the linear dynamic range, and use the fitted equation to quantify unknowns.

nodes.name: Quantitative Chromatography (Internal Standard)
nodes.description: Quantitative chromatography spikes a sample with an internal standard, separates and detects the analytes, confirms baseline resolution, integrates peak areas, applies response factors relative to the internal standard, and converts to concentration via calibration.

nodes.name: Potentiometry (Ion-Selective Electrode)
nodes.description: Potentiometry measures an ion's activity at zero current: a selective membrane develops a potential against a reference electrode following the Nernst/Nikolsky equation, and after calibration against standards the measured EMF is converted to concentration.

nodes.name: Method Validation (QC)
nodes.description: Validating an analytical method demonstrates fitness for purpose by establishing specificity, linearity and range, accuracy and precision, and detection/quantitation limits; failing criteria trigger method adjustment, and robustness plus system suitability finalize validation.

nodes.name: Sample Preparation Workflow
nodes.description: Sample preparation turns a raw sample into an analysis-ready solution: take a representative subsample, digest solids or extract/clean up liquids, remove matrix interferences, adjust to the working concentration range, then preserve and label the aliquot.

nodes.name: Beer–Lambert Quantitation
nodes.description: Quantitative spectrophotometry chooses an analytical wavelength, builds a calibration from standards, verifies Beer's-law linearity, fits a calibration line, then measures the sample absorbance and interpolates its concentration.

nodes.name: Michaelis-Menten Enzyme Kinetics
nodes.description: Michaelis-Menten kinetics models an enzyme binding substrate into an ES complex that turns over to product; under the steady-state assumption that [ES] is constant, the rate becomes v = Vmax[S]/(Km+[S]), and varying substrate concentration lets Km and Vmax be fitted from the saturation curve.

nodes.name: Glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas)
nodes.description: Glycolysis converts glucose to two pyruvate through an energy-investment phase that spends ATP to make fructose-1,6-bisphosphate, cleavage to two triose phosphates, and a payoff phase that oxidizes glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate to generate NADH and substrate-level ATP, requiring NAD+ regeneration to continue.

nodes.name: Protein Folding (Anfinsen)
nodes.description: Protein folding builds higher-order structure from the primary sequence: local secondary structure forms, hydrophobic collapse drives a tertiary fold toward the native free-energy minimum down a folding funnel, subunits assemble into quaternary structure, and misfolded chains are refolded with chaperones to reach the functional native state.

nodes.name: Molecular Docking (Virtual Screening)
nodes.description: Molecular docking predicts how ligands bind a target: the receptor is prepared, ligand poses are generated and placed in the binding site, scored for affinity, resampled when poor, ranked, and iterated over a library to shortlist virtual-screening hits.

nodes.name: Basis Set & Method Selection
nodes.description: Choosing a computational protocol balances accuracy against cost: pick a method family (DFT vs correlated wavefunction), select a basis set with appropriate polarization/diffuse functions for the chemistry, and benchmark against reference data before committing.

nodes.name: Geometry Optimization
nodes.description: Geometry optimization finds a stationary point by iteratively stepping along energy gradients until they vanish; a subsequent frequency calculation confirms a true minimum (no imaginary modes) or signals a saddle that must be re-optimized.

nodes.name: Free Energy (FEP / TI)
nodes.description: Alchemical free-energy methods compute a free-energy difference by coupling two end states through a parameter lambda: simulations at intermediate windows are run until sampling converges, and dU/dlambda or energy differences are integrated to give the free-energy change.

nodes.name: QM/MM Multiscale Modeling
nodes.description: QM/MM partitions a large system into a small quantum region treated with electronic structure and a classical force-field environment; link atoms handle the boundary and electrostatic embedding couples the regions, enabling reactions in enzymes or solution.

nodes.name: Lithium-Ion Battery Operation
nodes.description: A lithium-ion cell shuttles Li+ between a graphite anode and a layered-oxide cathode: during discharge lithium leaves the anode and intercalates into the cathode while electrons power the load, and charging reverses the flow, with SEI growth driving aging.

nodes.name: Computational Electrochemistry (DFT)
nodes.description: Computational electrocatalysis models an electrode reaction with DFT and the computational hydrogen electrode: intermediate energies build a potential-dependent free-energy diagram, identifying the potential-determining step and theoretical overpotential to screen catalysts.

nodes.name: Galvanic Corrosion Mechanism
nodes.description: Electrochemical corrosion couples an anodic metal-dissolution site to a cathodic reduction site through electron flow in the metal and ion flow in the electrolyte; galvanic coupling accelerates attack while a passive oxide film can slow it.

nodes.name: Cyclic Voltammetry
nodes.description: Cyclic voltammetry sweeps the electrode potential forward and back at a three-electrode cell, producing diffusion-limited current peaks; peak separation and scan-rate dependence reveal reversibility, redox potentials, electron count and kinetics.

nodes.name: Galvanic Cell & the Nernst Equation
nodes.description: Design a galvanic cell by assigning anode and cathode half-reactions, computing the standard cell potential, correcting for non-standard conditions with the Nernst equation, and judging spontaneity from the sign of the cell potential.

nodes.name: Electrodeposition: Nucleation & Growth
nodes.description: At an applied overpotential, metal ions are reduced to adatoms that diffuse on the surface; only clusters exceeding a critical size survive to grow, following instantaneous or progressive nucleation before coalescing into a continuous film.

nodes.name: Electrolysis of Water and Brine
nodes.description: Electrolysis uses an external voltage above the decomposition potential to drive reduction at the cathode and oxidation at the anode; competing anode reactions (O2 vs Cl2) depend on overpotential, and Faraday's laws relate product amounts to charge passed.

nodes.name: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy
nodes.description: EIS applies a small AC perturbation across a range of frequencies, measures the complex impedance, and fits Nyquist/Bode data to an equivalent circuit to extract charge-transfer resistance, double-layer capacitance and diffusional (Warburg) behavior.

nodes.name: Electroplating
nodes.description: Electroplating reduces metal cations from a bath onto a cathodic workpiece; adatoms diffuse, nucleate and grow into a coating, while a sacrificial anode of the plating metal dissolves to replenish the bath and additives control deposit uniformity.

nodes.name: PEM Fuel Cell Operation
nodes.description: A proton-exchange-membrane fuel cell oxidizes hydrogen at the anode, conducts protons through the membrane while electrons do work in the external circuit, and reduces oxygen at the cathode; the sluggish oxygen reduction is accelerated by a Pt catalyst.

nodes.name: Pourbaix (E–pH) Diagram Construction
nodes.description: A Pourbaix diagram maps the thermodynamically stable species of an element in water as a function of potential and pH: redox-only equilibria give horizontal lines, acid-base equilibria vertical lines, and combined proton-electron equilibria sloped Nernst lines.

nodes.name: Electrocatalytic Oxygen Reduction Pathway
nodes.description: The oxygen reduction reaction can follow a four-electron path to water or a two-electron path to peroxide; the rate is governed by intermediate binding energies, and the Sabatier optimum (volcano peak) gives the lowest overpotential.

nodes.name: Spectroelectrochemistry
nodes.description: Spectroelectrochemistry couples potential control with in-situ spectroscopy: stepping the potential in an optically transparent cell generates redox species whose UV-Vis, IR or EPR signatures are recorded simultaneously to correlate spectra with oxidation states and intermediates.

nodes.name: Secondary Organic Aerosol Formation
nodes.description: Secondary organic aerosol forms when volatile organic compounds are oxidized by OH, ozone, or nitrate radicals into lower-volatility products; once these vapors are supersaturated they partition onto existing particles or nucleate new ones, then grow by condensation and aging to build atmospheric aerosol mass.

nodes.name: Tropospheric Ozone (Photochemical Smog)
nodes.description: Tropospheric ozone builds through the photochemical NOx cycle: NO2 photolysis makes ozone while ozone titrates NO back to NO2 in a null cycle, but VOC oxidation produces peroxy radicals that convert NO to NO2 without consuming ozone, so net ozone accumulates and depends on the VOC-to-NOx balance.

nodes.name: Green Chemistry Process Design
nodes.description: Green chemistry redesigns a synthesis around its twelve principles: a candidate route is scored for atom economy, safer solvents and reagents are chosen, and the E-factor (mass of waste per mass of product) is estimated; the route is iterated with catalysis and waste reduction until both metrics are acceptable.

nodes.name: Soil Contaminant Remediation
nodes.description: Remediating contaminated soil begins with site assessment to map the contaminant and plume, then branches by whether the contaminant is dissolved or sorbed: dissolved plumes are pumped and treated while sorbed contaminants are mobilized or degraded in situ, with treatment iterated until concentrations fall below cleanup targets.

nodes.name: Carbonate System & Alkalinity
nodes.description: Natural water chemistry is governed by the carbonate system: dissolved CO2 forms carbonic acid that dissociates stepwise to bicarbonate and carbonate, setting speciation versus pH; treating the system as open (fixed pCO2) or closed (fixed total carbon) and applying mass and charge balance yields the equilibrium pH and alkalinity.

nodes.name: Coagulation-Flocculation Treatment
nodes.description: Coagulation-flocculation clarifies turbid water: a metal-salt coagulant is rapidly mixed to neutralize colloid surface charge and destabilize particles, then slow mixing promotes collisions that grow flocs; if flocs are large enough they settle out, otherwise aids are added and mixing continues before sedimentation yields clarified water.

nodes.name: Catalyst Design Influence Network
nodes.description: Computational catalyst design links structure to performance through reactivity descriptors: the active-site structure sets the d-band center, which shifts adsorption energy; linear scaling relations tie adsorption energy to activation barriers and turnover frequency, and the Sabatier principle predicts an optimal intermediate binding that, balanced against selectivity and stability, determines overall catalyst performance.

nodes.name: Organometallic Catalytic Cycle
nodes.description: Homogeneous organometallic catalysis turns over through elementary organometallic steps - substrate coordination, oxidative addition, migratory insertion, and reductive elimination - with the 16/18-electron rule governing whether a vacant site is available for the next step.

nodes.name: Crystal Field Theory
nodes.description: Crystal field theory models ligands as point charges that split a metal's d-orbitals according to coordination geometry; comparing the splitting to the electron pairing energy predicts high- or low-spin configurations, color, and magnetic properties.

nodes.name: Coordination Complex Synthesis
nodes.description: Synthesizing a coordination complex selects solvent and conditions so ligands displace labile aqua/halide ligands; the chelate effect favors stable polydentate products, and the target geometry/isomer is reached by tuning stoichiometry or templating before crystallization.

nodes.name: Ligand Substitution Mechanisms
nodes.description: Ligand substitution at a metal center proceeds by associative or dissociative pathways distinguished by the rate law: associative paths form a higher-coordinate intermediate by binding the incoming ligand first, while dissociative paths first lose a ligand to a lower-coordinate intermediate.

nodes.name: Metal Oxidation (Ellingham)
nodes.description: Metal oxidation is analyzed with an Ellingham diagram of the oxidation free energy versus temperature: a negative free energy means the oxide is favored, and a reductant whose oxidation line lies below the metal's allows the oxide to be reduced (e.g. carbothermic reduction).

nodes.name: Born–Haber Cycle
nodes.description: The Born-Haber cycle applies Hess's law to ionic-solid formation, summing atomization, ionization, electron affinity and lattice enthalpy; comparing the cycle's predicted formation enthalpy with experiment lets one solve for or validate the lattice energy.

nodes.name: Arrhenius Plot for Activation Energy
nodes.description: Extract the activation energy and pre-exponential factor by measuring rate constants at several temperatures, linearizing the Arrhenius equation as ln k versus 1/T, and reading the activation energy from the slope and the pre-factor from the intercept.

nodes.name: Catalysis Lowers Activation Energy
nodes.description: A catalyst opens an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy by binding reactants and stabilizing the transition state, accelerating the rate and being regenerated, without changing the position of equilibrium.

nodes.name: Rate Law from Concentration
nodes.description: Determine a reaction's rate law by the method of initial rates: vary reactant concentrations, measure initial rates, deduce the order in each reactant, then assemble the rate law and solve for the rate constant.

nodes.name: Pressure Effects on Gas Reactions
nodes.description: Raising pressure increases gas-phase partial pressures and collision frequency, accelerating bimolecular reactions; unimolecular reactions show Lindemann fall-off where the apparent order changes with pressure, and changes in gas moles shift equilibrium.

nodes.name: Mechanism Elucidation from Probes
nodes.description: Elucidate a mechanism by combining experimental probes - the measured rate law, trapped intermediates, kinetic isotope effects, and stereochemical outcome - proposing a mechanism, testing it against all evidence and computation, and iterating.

nodes.name: Elementary Steps & Rate-Determining Step
nodes.description: Build a mechanism from elementary steps and intermediates, derive the rate law either from a clear rate-determining step or via the steady-state approximation, and accept the mechanism only if its predicted rate law matches experiment.

nodes.name: Collision Theory & Temperature
nodes.description: Collision theory explains temperature dependence: only collisions with energy above the activation energy and correct orientation react, and raising temperature broadens the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution so a larger fraction of collisions is effective.

nodes.name: Composite Fabrication
nodes.description: Composite fabrication combines a matrix with a reinforcing phase: after selecting matrix chemistry, fibers or particles are surface-treated for wetting, then impregnated or infiltrated by the matrix, and interfacial bonding is verified before consolidation and curing produce the finished part.

nodes.name: Nanoparticle Synthesis (LaMer)
nodes.description: The LaMer model explains monodisperse nanoparticle synthesis: precursor decomposition raises monomer concentration until a critical supersaturation triggers a single burst of nucleation, which drops the concentration below threshold so no new nuclei form, and the existing nuclei grow by diffusion under capping-ligand control into uniform particles.

nodes.name: Controlled Radical Polymerization (ATRP/RAFT)
nodes.description: Controlled/living radical polymerization keeps a low instantaneous radical concentration through a fast dynamic equilibrium between active and dormant chains; when the equilibrium strongly favors the dormant species, all chains grow at the same slow rate, giving predictable molar mass and low dispersity once the target is reached.

nodes.name: Emulsion Polymerization
nodes.description: Emulsion polymerization builds polymer inside surfactant micelles dispersed in water: above the critical micelle concentration a water-soluble initiator generates radicals that enter micelles to nucleate particles, monomer diffuses from droplets to feed propagation, and growth continues until droplets are exhausted, yielding a polymer latex.

nodes.name: Sol-Gel Synthesis
nodes.description: Sol-gel processing converts metal alkoxide precursors into an oxide network through hydrolysis and condensation: a colloidal sol crosslinks into a continuous gel, which is aged and then dried either supercritically to an aerogel or by evaporation to a xerogel, and finally calcined to the dense oxide.

nodes.name: Thin-Film Deposition by CVD
nodes.description: Chemical vapor deposition grows a solid thin film from gaseous precursors: reactant gases are transported to a heated substrate where they adsorb, react or decompose, nucleate, and grow into a conformal film while volatile byproducts desorb, with deposition rate and uniformity controlled by temperature, pressure, and flow.

nodes.name: Composite Stiffness (Rule of Mixtures)
nodes.description: The rule of mixtures predicts the elastic stiffness of a continuous-fiber composite from constituent properties and volume fractions: loading parallel to the fibers obeys the iso-strain Voigt bound while transverse loading obeys the iso-stress Reuss bound, with corrections for fiber orientation giving the effective modulus.

nodes.name: Crystal Structure & Unit Cells
nodes.description: Determining a crystal structure starts from the lattice-and-basis description: a unit cell and its Bravais lattice are identified, close-packed arrangements are classified as FCC/HCP versus BCC, coordination number and atomic packing factor are computed, and Miller indices label planes for relating structure to properties.

nodes.name: Doping & Point Defects
nodes.description: Doping a semiconductor introduces substitutional impurities that act as donors or acceptors: thermal ionization releases electrons or holes, charge neutrality is maintained through compensating defects described by Kroger-Vink notation, and the resulting carrier concentration sets the conductivity and type of the doped material.

nodes.name: Quantum Confinement in Nanocrystals
nodes.description: When a semiconductor crystal shrinks below the exciton Bohr radius, charge carriers become spatially confined, their energy levels become discrete, and the band gap widens with decreasing size; this size-tunable gap shifts the absorption and emission, allowing the synthesis size to be adjusted to hit a target wavelength.

nodes.name: Polymorphism & Phase Transformation
nodes.description: Polymorphic systems can crystallize into several structures: kinetics often favor a metastable form first (Ostwald's rule of stages) while thermodynamics favor the most stable polymorph, and applying temperature, pressure, or a seed crosses the transformation barrier to convert toward the controlled target polymorph.

nodes.name: Fragment-Based Drug Discovery
nodes.description: Fragment-based discovery screens a library of small fragments by biophysical methods, confirms weak binders and their binding site by structure, then grows or links fragments through iterative medicinal chemistry to build a high-affinity lead.

nodes.name: Dose–Response & EC50
nodes.description: Pharmacodynamic characterization: apply a range of doses, measure the response, fit a sigmoidal concentration-response (Hill) curve to extract potency (EC50/IC50) and efficacy, and assess cooperativity and the therapeutic window.

nodes.name: Structure–Activity Relationship Cycle
nodes.description: An SAR optimization cycle: hypothesize how structure affects activity, design and synthesize analogues, assay potency and selectivity, update the SAR model when activity is not yet optimal, and converge on a pharmacophore and optimized candidate.

nodes.name: Prodrug Activation
nodes.description: A prodrug is administered inactive, absorbed and distributed, then converted to the active drug at the target site by enzymatic or chemical triggers; the active species binds its target to produce the effect before being metabolized and cleared.

nodes.name: Hit-to-Lead ADMET Optimization
nodes.description: Hit-to-lead progression: confirm and triage hits, assess potency, profile ADMET, and iteratively modify structure to satisfy drug-likeness, improve solubility and metabolic stability, and clear toxicity flags before nominating a lead series.

nodes.name: Radiocarbon Dating
nodes.description: Radiocarbon dating exploits the fixed atmospheric 14C/12C ratio in living tissue: when an organism dies, uptake stops and 14C decays with a 5730-year half-life, so measuring the residual 14C and calibrating against tree-ring records yields a calendar age.

nodes.name: Radioactive Decay Kinetics
nodes.description: An unstable nuclide decays by a mode set by its neutron-to-proton ratio (beta-minus, beta-plus/electron capture, or alpha), emitting particles and energy; decay follows first-order kinetics with a characteristic half-life, sometimes through a chain to a stable nuclide.

nodes.name: Nuclear Fission Chain Reaction
nodes.description: A fissile nucleus absorbs a neutron, deforms and splits into fragments, releasing energy and two to three neutrons; whether the chain is self-sustaining depends on neutron moderation and losses, captured by the effective multiplication factor.

nodes.name: Radioisotope Production (Neutron Activation)
nodes.description: Neutron activation produces radioisotopes by irradiating a stable target so it captures neutrons via (n,γ) to form a radioactive nuclide; after sufficient activity is reached, the product is chemically separated, purified and used as a tracer or radiopharmaceutical.

nodes.name: Nuclear Fusion (D–T)
nodes.description: Fusion joins light nuclei into a heavier one: only when enough energy is supplied to tunnel through the Coulomb barrier do deuterium and tritium fuse, releasing energy from the mass defect, and net power requires meeting the Lawson confinement criterion.

nodes.name: Aldol Condensation
nodes.description: In the aldol reaction an enol/enolate generated from a carbonyl with an alpha-hydrogen attacks a second carbonyl to give a beta-hydroxy carbonyl; on heating, E1cb dehydration eliminates water to form a conjugated alpha,beta-unsaturated product.

nodes.name: Fischer Esterification
nodes.description: Acid-catalyzed Fischer esterification condenses a carboxylic acid and an alcohol: protonation activates the carbonyl, the alcohol adds to form a tetrahedral intermediate, and loss of water gives the ester; the reaction is an equilibrium driven by removing water or using excess reagent.

nodes.name: Grignard Reaction
nodes.description: A Grignard reagent forms when magnesium inserts into a carbon-halogen bond, giving a strongly nucleophilic carbanion equivalent that adds to electrophilic carbonyls (or CO2); aqueous workup then protonates the alkoxide to give an alcohol or carboxylic acid.

nodes.name: Nucleophilic Substitution: SN1 vs SN2
nodes.description: Whether an aliphatic nucleophilic substitution follows SN1 or SN2 depends mainly on substrate class: methyl/primary substrates favor concerted backside SN2 with inversion, tertiary favor stepwise SN1 via a carbocation with racemization, and secondary depend on conditions.

nodes.name: Retrosynthetic Analysis
nodes.description: Retrosynthesis works backward from a target: identify functional groups, make strategic bond disconnections into synthons and their reagent equivalents, check that each maps to a known reaction, and recurse on the precursors until commercially available starting materials are reached.

nodes.name: R/S Stereochemistry Assignment
nodes.description: Assign absolute configuration with the Cahn-Ingold-Prelog rules: locate each stereocenter, rank its four substituents by priority, orient the lowest priority away, and read 1->2->3 as clockwise (R) or counterclockwise (S), iterating over all centers.

nodes.name: Suzuki–Miyaura Coupling
nodes.description: The palladium-catalyzed Suzuki–Miyaura coupling joins an aryl halide and an organoboron compound through a catalytic cycle of oxidative addition, base-assisted transmetalation, and reductive elimination, regenerating the Pd(0) catalyst each turn.

nodes.name: Rate Law by Initial Rates
nodes.description: The method of initial rates determines a rate law empirically: a series of experiments varies one reactant concentration at a time while measuring the initial rate, the order in each reactant is read from how the rate scales, and once all orders are known the overall rate law is assembled and the rate constant is fitted.

nodes.name: Phase Diagram (Gibbs Phase Rule)
nodes.description: A phase diagram is constructed from the Gibbs phase rule F = C - P + 2, which fixes the degrees of freedom: single-phase regions and the boundaries between them are mapped on temperature-composition axes, the lever rule gives phase amounts inside two-phase regions, and invariant points complete the diagram.

nodes.name: Measuring Reaction Rates
nodes.description: Reaction rates are measured by tracking a concentration-dependent observable over time: the technique is chosen by reaction speed (stopped-flow mixing for fast reactions, periodic sampling for slow ones), the recorded signal is converted to concentration, and tangents or integrated fits yield the instantaneous rate.

nodes.name: Spectral Line Broadening
nodes.description: An observed spectral line is broadened by several mechanisms: finite state lifetimes give a Lorentzian natural width, thermal motion adds a Gaussian Doppler width, and collisions add pressure broadening; the dominant mechanism sets the limiting shape, and convolving Gaussian and Lorentzian contributions yields the Voigt profile.

nodes.name: Beer-Lambert Quantitative Analysis
nodes.description: Quantitative absorption spectroscopy relies on the Beer-Lambert law A = epsilon*b*c: a wavelength near the absorption maximum is selected, absorbance is kept within the linear range by dilution, a calibration curve is built from standards, and the unknown concentration is interpolated from its measured absorbance.

nodes.name: Hess's Law Thermochemical Cycle
nodes.description: Because enthalpy is a state function, a reaction enthalpy can be obtained indirectly via Hess's law: if standard formation enthalpies are available they are combined directly, otherwise a thermochemical cycle of known reactions is built and scaled, summed, and cross-checked against bond enthalpies to give the reaction enthalpy.

nodes.name: Step-Growth Polymerization (Carothers)
nodes.description: Step-growth polymerization builds high molecular weight only at high conversion: difunctional monomers condense, the Carothers equation links degree of polymerization to extent of reaction, and removing the condensation byproduct drives the equilibrium forward.

nodes.name: Entropic Rubber Elasticity
nodes.description: In a cross-linked elastomer the restoring force is entropic: stretching extends network chains and lowers their conformational entropy, so the chains recoil when released, a behavior captured by the statistical theory of rubber elasticity.

nodes.name: Glass Transition in Polymers
nodes.description: On cooling an amorphous polymer, free volume contracts until, below the glass transition temperature, segmental motion freezes and the material becomes a rigid glass; cooling rate and plasticizer content shift the observed Tg.

nodes.name: Flory–Huggins Solution Theory
nodes.description: Flory–Huggins lattice theory combines a small combinatorial entropy of mixing for long chains with an enthalpic χ interaction parameter; the resulting free energy predicts miscibility or phase separation of polymer solutions.

nodes.name: Free-Radical Chain Polymerization
nodes.description: Free-radical chain polymerization proceeds by initiator decomposition, repeated monomer addition during propagation, and chain-end loss by combination/disproportionation or chain transfer, governing molecular weight and conversion.

nodes.name: Ab Initio Hartree–Fock SCF
nodes.description: Hartree–Fock solves the electronic structure self-consistently: from a geometry and basis set, integrals are computed, an initial guess builds the Fock matrix, the Roothaan equations are solved for new orbitals and density, and the cycle repeats until the energy converges.

nodes.name: Kohn–Sham DFT Workflow
nodes.description: Kohn–Sham DFT recasts the interacting electron problem as non-interacting electrons in an effective potential: after choosing an exchange-correlation functional and basis, the density is iterated through the KS equations until self-consistency, then properties are computed.

nodes.name: Electronic Excited States (TD-DFT)
nodes.description: Excited states are computed from the ground state with time-dependent DFT: solving the Casida equations yields excitation energies and oscillator strengths, selection rules classify transitions as bright or dark, and Franck–Condon factors shape the simulated UV-Vis spectrum.

nodes.name: Molecular Dynamics Setup
nodes.description: Setting up an MD simulation: define the system and force field, build and minimize the configuration, assign velocities and equilibrate under a thermostat/barostat, then run production dynamics with a stable integration timestep before analyzing the trajectory.

nodes.name: Molecular Orbitals via LCAO
nodes.description: Molecular orbitals are built from atomic orbitals: only AOs of matching symmetry and similar energy combine, generating bonding and antibonding MOs that are ordered by energy and filled by the Aufbau principle to give bond order and an MO diagram.

nodes.name: Transition State on the PES
nodes.description: Locating a reaction pathway on the potential energy surface: build an initial path between reactant and product, optimize it (e.g. nudged elastic band) to a first-order saddle point, verify it has a single imaginary frequency, and confirm by IRC before reading the activation energy.

nodes.name: Harmonic Vibrational (IR) Frequencies
nodes.description: Vibrational frequencies follow from the Hessian at an optimized geometry: mass-weighting and diagonalization give normal modes; the absence of imaginary frequencies confirms a minimum, IR intensities come from dipole derivatives, and scaled frequencies simulate the IR spectrum.

nodes.name: Chromatographic Separation
nodes.description: Chromatography separates a mixture by differential partitioning between a mobile and a stationary phase: components with stronger stationary-phase affinity are retained longer; band broadening (van Deemter) limits resolution, which is tuned by adjusting phase and flow.

nodes.name: FT-IR Spectroscopy
nodes.description: FT-IR passes broadband infrared light through an interferometer and sample; bonds whose dipole moment changes absorb at characteristic frequencies, the interferogram is Fourier-transformed to a spectrum, and functional-group and fingerprint regions identify the compound.

nodes.name: Mass Spectrometry
nodes.description: Mass spectrometry ionizes a sample to molecular and fragment ions, accelerates them into a mass analyzer that separates by mass-to-charge ratio, and detects abundances; identifying the molecular ion and interpreting fragmentation reveals mass and structure.

nodes.name: NMR Spectroscopy
nodes.description: In NMR, nuclei in a magnetic field are excited by an RF pulse; the recorded free-induction decay is Fourier-transformed to a spectrum where chemical shifts report electronic environment and spin-spin coupling splits signals, while integration counts equivalent nuclei.

nodes.name: Raman Spectroscopy
nodes.description: Raman spectroscopy detects inelastically scattered laser light: modes that change polarizability produce Stokes/anti-Stokes shifts after the intense Rayleigh line is filtered, giving a vibrational fingerprint that complements IR through the rule of mutual exclusion.

nodes.name: UV-Vis Spectroscopy
nodes.description: UV-Vis measures electronic absorption: light is scanned through a sample, chromophores promote electrons (pi->pi*, n->pi*), the absorbance is computed from transmitted intensity, and within the linear range Beer's law relates absorbance to concentration.

nodes.name: X-ray Diffraction Analysis
nodes.description: X-ray diffraction probes crystal structure: X-rays scatter off lattice planes and interfere constructively only when Bragg's law is satisfied, producing peaks versus 2-theta that are indexed and refined (e.g. Rietveld) to determine the unit cell and phase.

nodes.name: Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm
nodes.description: The Langmuir model treats adsorption as molecules occupying a finite set of equivalent surface sites: molecules that strike an empty site adsorb, adsorption and desorption reach equilibrium, and coverage follows theta = KP/(1+KP), saturating at a monolayer.

nodes.name: Physisorption vs Chemisorption
nodes.description: Adsorption on a surface is classified by the interaction: weak van der Waals attraction gives reversible physisorption, while electron sharing gives chemisorption with a chemical bond, which may be activated (with a barrier) or non-activated.

nodes.name: Surface Reconstruction & Relaxation
nodes.description: A freshly created surface has high-energy dangling bonds; to lower its energy the top layers relax (change spacing) or reconstruct (rebond into a new periodicity), behavior that adsorbates can further alter and that is probed by LEED or STM.

nodes.name: Surface Active Sites & Structure Sensitivity
nodes.description: Catalytic activity often depends on surface site type: terraces, steps and kinks differ in coordination, with low-coordination defect sites usually more reactive; whether the rate depends on site distribution defines a structure-sensitive versus structure-insensitive reaction.

nodes.name: Self-Assembled Monolayers (SAMs)
nodes.description: Self-assembled monolayers form when amphiphilic molecules chemisorb through their headgroups onto a surface (e.g. thiols on gold) and then order via tail-tail van der Waals packing; coverage builds with time and annealing removes defects to yield a functional monolayer.

nodes.name: Heterogeneous Catalysis (Langmuir–Hinshelwood)
nodes.description: In the Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism both reactants adsorb on the catalyst surface, diffuse and react in the adsorbed state, and the product desorbs to regenerate the active site, giving a catalytic turnover cycle.

nodes.name: Catalyst Screening (Volcano)
nodes.description: Catalyst screening ranks candidates by adsorbate binding energy against the Sabatier principle: too-strong binding poisons the surface and too-weak binding fails to activate reactants, so the best catalyst sits near the volcano peak with intermediate binding.

nodes.name: BET Surface Area Analysis
nodes.description: BET analysis measures specific surface area from multilayer gas physisorption: after degassing, nitrogen is dosed at increasing relative pressures, the BET equation is fit over its linear region to extract monolayer capacity, and the surface area follows from molecular cross-section.

nodes.name: Semiconductor Photocatalysis (TiO2)
nodes.description: In TiO2 photocatalysis, photons above the band gap excite electron-hole pairs; charges either recombine (losing energy) or migrate to the surface, where they drive redox reactions that generate reactive oxygen species and degrade adsorbed pollutants.

nodes.name: Wetting & Contact Angle
nodes.description: Wetting is governed by the balance of solid-vapor, solid-liquid and liquid-vapor interfacial tensions through Young's equation; the resulting contact angle classifies wetting, complete spreading or non-wetting, and roughness modifies it via the Wenzel/Cassie-Baxter regimes.

nodes.name: Supported Metal Catalyst Preparation
nodes.description: Supported metal catalysts are made by impregnating a high-area oxide support with a metal precursor, drying, calcining to the oxide, and reducing to metal nanoparticles; dispersion is measured by chemisorption and tuned by adjusting loading and conditions.

nodes.name: Zeolite Shape-Selective Catalysis
nodes.description: Zeolites impose shape selectivity through their micropores: reactants must fit the aperture to reach Brønsted acid sites, the transition state must fit the cavity, and only products that can exit are released, giving reactant, transition-state and product selectivity.

nodes.name: Transition State Theory (Eyring)
nodes.description: Transition state theory treats reactants as in quasi-equilibrium with an activated complex at the saddle point of the potential energy surface; the Eyring equation converts the activation free energy into a rate constant, with corrections for recrossing and tunneling.

nodes.name: Marcus Theory of Electron Transfer
nodes.description: Marcus theory describes outer-sphere electron transfer with parabolic free-energy curves for reactants and products; the activation free energy depends on the reorganization energy and driving force, predicting the celebrated inverted region at large driving force.

nodes.name: Born–Oppenheimer Approximation
nodes.description: Because nuclei are far heavier than electrons, the Born-Oppenheimer approximation separates nuclear and electronic motion: electrons are solved for fixed nuclei to give a potential energy surface on which nuclei then move, valid unless electronic states approach degeneracy.

nodes.name: Rayleigh–Schrödinger Perturbation Theory
nodes.description: Perturbation theory treats a Hamiltonian as a solvable reference plus a small perturbation, expanding the energy and wavefunction in powers of the coupling; first- and second-order corrections (as in Møller–Plesset MP2) improve the energy when the series converges.

nodes.name: The Variational Method
nodes.description: The variational principle guarantees that the expectation value of the Hamiltonian for any trial wavefunction is an upper bound to the true ground-state energy; minimizing over adjustable parameters and improving the trial form drives the estimate toward the exact energy.

nodes.name: Chemical Equilibrium & Le Chatelier
nodes.description: Starting from a system at equilibrium, apply a disturbance (composition, temperature, or pressure), compare the reaction quotient to K, and predict the direction of the shift that relieves the stress until a new equilibrium is reached.

nodes.name: Chemical Potential & Phase Equilibrium
nodes.description: Express each species' chemical potential as a function of activity, then drive multiphase systems toward equilibrium by transferring matter down the chemical-potential gradient until the potential of every component is equal in all phases.

nodes.name: Entropy Change & the Second Law
nodes.description: Compute the entropy change of a process via a reversible path, add the surroundings' entropy change, and use the sign of the total entropy of the universe to judge whether the process is spontaneous, reversible, or impossible.

nodes.name: Gibbs Free Energy & Spontaneity
nodes.description: Determine reaction spontaneity by combining enthalpy and entropy into the Gibbs free energy change, classifying the sign of ΔG and relating ΔG° to the equilibrium constant.

nodes.name: First-Order Phase Transition (Clapeyron)
nodes.description: Two phases reach coexistence when their chemical potentials are equal; the Clapeyron equation then relates the slope of the phase boundary to the entropy and volume changes, and crossing the boundary drives the transition with its latent heat.

nodes.name: Ideal vs Real Solution Behavior
nodes.description: Model a mixture starting from ideal mixing, test for Raoult's-law behavior, and when deviations occur describe them with activity coefficients and an excess Gibbs energy to predict vapor pressures and phase equilibria.

nodes.name: Reaction Enthalpy via Hess's Law
nodes.description: Obtain a reaction enthalpy either by direct calorimetry or, when the reaction is impractical to measure, by combining tabulated enthalpies of known reactions or formation enthalpies through Hess's law.

variants.label: Curated Lens Map
variants.description: Uses collection presets as conceptual lenses, letting processes appear under task-oriented groupings.

rubric.notes: First-pass heuristic score for chemistry; revise after visual inspection.

nodes.name: Whole of Chemistry
nodes.description: Reactions, measurements, synthesis workflows, materials, and molecular models.

nodes.name: Reaction Mechanisms
nodes.description: Bond formation, bond breaking, catalytic cycles, and mechanistic transformations.

nodes.name: Free Energy (FEP / TI)
nodes.description: Alchemical free-energy methods compute a free-energy difference by coupling two end states through a parameter lambda: simulations at intermediate windows are run until sampling converges, and dU/dlambda or energy differences are integrated to give the free-energy change.

nodes.name: QM/MM Multiscale Modeling
nodes.description: QM/MM partitions a large system into a small quantum region treated with electronic structure and a classical force-field environment; link atoms handle the boundary and electrostatic embedding couples the regions, enabling reactions in enzymes or solution.

nodes.name: Computational Electrochemistry (DFT)
nodes.description: Computational electrocatalysis models an electrode reaction with DFT and the computational hydrogen electrode: intermediate energies build a potential-dependent free-energy diagram, identifying the potential-determining step and theoretical overpotential to screen catalysts.

nodes.name: Galvanic Corrosion Mechanism
nodes.description: Electrochemical corrosion couples an anodic metal-dissolution site to a cathodic reduction site through electron flow in the metal and ion flow in the electrolyte; galvanic coupling accelerates attack while a passive oxide film can slow it.

nodes.name: Galvanic Cell & the Nernst Equation
nodes.description: Design a galvanic cell by assigning anode and cathode half-reactions, computing the standard cell potential, correcting for non-standard conditions with the Nernst equation, and judging spontaneity from the sign of the cell potential.

nodes.name: Electrocatalytic Oxygen Reduction Pathway
nodes.description: The oxygen reduction reaction can follow a four-electron path to water or a two-electron path to peroxide; the rate is governed by intermediate binding energies, and the Sabatier optimum (volcano peak) gives the lowest overpotential.

nodes.name: Secondary Organic Aerosol Formation
nodes.description: Secondary organic aerosol forms when volatile organic compounds are oxidized by OH, ozone, or nitrate radicals into lower-volatility products; once these vapors are supersaturated they partition onto existing particles or nucleate new ones, then grow by condensation and aging to build atmospheric aerosol mass.

nodes.name: Green Chemistry Process Design
nodes.description: Green chemistry redesigns a synthesis around its twelve principles: a candidate route is scored for atom economy, safer solvents and reagents are chosen, and the E-factor (mass of waste per mass of product) is estimated; the route is iterated with catalysis and waste reduction until both metrics are acceptable.

nodes.name: Catalyst Design Influence Network
nodes.description: Computational catalyst design links structure to performance through reactivity descriptors: the active-site structure sets the d-band center, which shifts adsorption energy; linear scaling relations tie adsorption energy to activation barriers and turnover frequency, and the Sabatier principle predicts an optimal intermediate binding that, balanced against selectivity and stability, determines overall catalyst performance.

nodes.name: Organometallic Catalytic Cycle
nodes.description: Homogeneous organometallic catalysis turns over through elementary organometallic steps - substrate coordination, oxidative addition, migratory insertion, and reductive elimination - with the 16/18-electron rule governing whether a vacant site is available for the next step.

nodes.name: Ligand Substitution Mechanisms
nodes.description: Ligand substitution at a metal center proceeds by associative or dissociative pathways distinguished by the rate law: associative paths form a higher-coordinate intermediate by binding the incoming ligand first, while dissociative paths first lose a ligand to a lower-coordinate intermediate.

nodes.name: Catalysis Lowers Activation Energy
nodes.description: A catalyst opens an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy by binding reactants and stabilizing the transition state, accelerating the rate and being regenerated, without changing the position of equilibrium.

nodes.name: Rate Law from Concentration
nodes.description: Determine a reaction's rate law by the method of initial rates: vary reactant concentrations, measure initial rates, deduce the order in each reactant, then assemble the rate law and solve for the rate constant.

nodes.name: Mechanism Elucidation from Probes
nodes.description: Elucidate a mechanism by combining experimental probes - the measured rate law, trapped intermediates, kinetic isotope effects, and stereochemical outcome - proposing a mechanism, testing it against all evidence and computation, and iterating.

nodes.name: Elementary Steps & Rate-Determining Step
nodes.description: Build a mechanism from elementary steps and intermediates, derive the rate law either from a clear rate-determining step or via the steady-state approximation, and accept the mechanism only if its predicted rate law matches experiment.

nodes.name: Sol-Gel Synthesis
nodes.description: Sol-gel processing converts metal alkoxide precursors into an oxide network through hydrolysis and condensation: a colloidal sol crosslinks into a continuous gel, which is aged and then dried either supercritically to an aerogel or by evaporation to a xerogel, and finally calcined to the dense oxide.

nodes.name: Thin-Film Deposition by CVD
nodes.description: Chemical vapor deposition grows a solid thin film from gaseous precursors: reactant gases are transported to a heated substrate where they adsorb, react or decompose, nucleate, and grow into a conformal film while volatile byproducts desorb, with deposition rate and uniformity controlled by temperature, pressure, and flow.

nodes.name: Prodrug Activation
nodes.description: A prodrug is administered inactive, absorbed and distributed, then converted to the active drug at the target site by enzymatic or chemical triggers; the active species binds its target to produce the effect before being metabolized and cleared.

nodes.name: Nuclear Fission Chain Reaction
nodes.description: A fissile nucleus absorbs a neutron, deforms and splits into fragments, releasing energy and two to three neutrons; whether the chain is self-sustaining depends on neutron moderation and losses, captured by the effective multiplication factor.

nodes.name: Aldol Condensation
nodes.description: In the aldol reaction an enol/enolate generated from a carbonyl with an alpha-hydrogen attacks a second carbonyl to give a beta-hydroxy carbonyl; on heating, E1cb dehydration eliminates water to form a conjugated alpha,beta-unsaturated product.

nodes.name: Fischer Esterification
nodes.description: Acid-catalyzed Fischer esterification condenses a carboxylic acid and an alcohol: protonation activates the carbonyl, the alcohol adds to form a tetrahedral intermediate, and loss of water gives the ester; the reaction is an equilibrium driven by removing water or using excess reagent.

nodes.name: Grignard Reaction
nodes.description: A Grignard reagent forms when magnesium inserts into a carbon-halogen bond, giving a strongly nucleophilic carbanion equivalent that adds to electrophilic carbonyls (or CO2); aqueous workup then protonates the alkoxide to give an alcohol or carboxylic acid.

nodes.name: Nucleophilic Substitution: SN1 vs SN2
nodes.description: Whether an aliphatic nucleophilic substitution follows SN1 or SN2 depends mainly on substrate class: methyl/primary substrates favor concerted backside SN2 with inversion, tertiary favor stepwise SN1 via a carbocation with racemization, and secondary depend on conditions.

nodes.name: Retrosynthetic Analysis
nodes.description: Retrosynthesis works backward from a target: identify functional groups, make strategic bond disconnections into synthons and their reagent equivalents, check that each maps to a known reaction, and recurse on the precursors until commercially available starting materials are reached.

nodes.name: Suzuki–Miyaura Coupling
nodes.description: The palladium-catalyzed Suzuki–Miyaura coupling joins an aryl halide and an organoboron compound through a catalytic cycle of oxidative addition, base-assisted transmetalation, and reductive elimination, regenerating the Pd(0) catalyst each turn.

nodes.name: Rate Law by Initial Rates
nodes.description: The method of initial rates determines a rate law empirically: a series of experiments varies one reactant concentration at a time while measuring the initial rate, the order in each reactant is read from how the rate scales, and once all orders are known the overall rate law is assembled and the rate constant is fitted.

nodes.name: Measuring Reaction Rates
nodes.description: Reaction rates are measured by tracking a concentration-dependent observable over time: the technique is chosen by reaction speed (stopped-flow mixing for fast reactions, periodic sampling for slow ones), the recorded signal is converted to concentration, and tangents or integrated fits yield the instantaneous rate.

nodes.name: Spectral Line Broadening
nodes.description: An observed spectral line is broadened by several mechanisms: finite state lifetimes give a Lorentzian natural width, thermal motion adds a Gaussian Doppler width, and collisions add pressure broadening; the dominant mechanism sets the limiting shape, and convolving Gaussian and Lorentzian contributions yields the Voigt profile.

nodes.name: Hess's Law Thermochemical Cycle
nodes.description: Because enthalpy is a state function, a reaction enthalpy can be obtained indirectly via Hess's law: if standard formation enthalpies are available they are combined directly, otherwise a thermochemical cycle of known reactions is built and scaled, summed, and cross-checked against bond enthalpies to give the reaction enthalpy.

nodes.name: Step-Growth Polymerization (Carothers)
nodes.description: Step-growth polymerization builds high molecular weight only at high conversion: difunctional monomers condense, the Carothers equation links degree of polymerization to extent of reaction, and removing the condensation byproduct drives the equilibrium forward.

nodes.name: Transition State on the PES
nodes.description: Locating a reaction pathway on the potential energy surface: build an initial path between reactant and product, optimize it (e.g. nudged elastic band) to a first-order saddle point, verify it has a single imaginary frequency, and confirm by IRC before reading the activation energy.

nodes.name: NMR Spectroscopy
nodes.description: In NMR, nuclei in a magnetic field are excited by an RF pulse; the recorded free-induction decay is Fourier-transformed to a spectrum where chemical shifts report electronic environment and spin-spin coupling splits signals, while integration counts equivalent nuclei.

nodes.name: Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm
nodes.description: The Langmuir model treats adsorption as molecules occupying a finite set of equivalent surface sites: molecules that strike an empty site adsorb, adsorption and desorption reach equilibrium, and coverage follows theta = KP/(1+KP), saturating at a monolayer.

nodes.name: Surface Active Sites & Structure Sensitivity
nodes.description: Catalytic activity often depends on surface site type: terraces, steps and kinks differ in coordination, with low-coordination defect sites usually more reactive; whether the rate depends on site distribution defines a structure-sensitive versus structure-insensitive reaction.

nodes.description: Self-assembled monolayers form when amphiphilic molecules chemisorb through their headgroups onto a surface (e.g. thiols on gold) and then order via tail-tail van der Waals packing; coverage builds with time and annealing removes defects to yield a functional monolayer.

nodes.description: Determine reaction spontaneity by combining enthalpy and entropy into the Gibbs free energy change, classifying the sign of ΔG and relating ΔG° to the equilibrium constant.

nodes.name: Reaction Enthalpy via Hess's Law
nodes.description: Obtain a reaction enthalpy either by direct calorimetry or, when the reaction is impractical to measure, by combining tabulated enthalpies of known reactions or formation enthalpies through Hess's law.

nodes.name: Thermodynamics and Kinetics
nodes.description: Free energy, equilibrium, chemical potential, rates, and Arrhenius behavior.

nodes.name: Potentiometry (Ion-Selective Electrode)
nodes.description: Potentiometry measures an ion's activity at zero current: a selective membrane develops a potential against a reference electrode following the Nernst/Nikolsky equation, and after calibration against standards the measured EMF is converted to concentration.

nodes.name: Michaelis-Menten Enzyme Kinetics
nodes.description: Michaelis-Menten kinetics models an enzyme binding substrate into an ES complex that turns over to product; under the steady-state assumption that [ES] is constant, the rate becomes v = Vmax[S]/(Km+[S]), and varying substrate concentration lets Km and Vmax be fitted from the saturation curve.

nodes.name: Cyclic Voltammetry
nodes.description: Cyclic voltammetry sweeps the electrode potential forward and back at a three-electrode cell, producing diffusion-limited current peaks; peak separation and scan-rate dependence reveal reversibility, redox potentials, electron count and kinetics.

nodes.name: Electrolysis of Water and Brine
nodes.description: Electrolysis uses an external voltage above the decomposition potential to drive reduction at the cathode and oxidation at the anode; competing anode reactions (O2 vs Cl2) depend on overpotential, and Faraday's laws relate product amounts to charge passed.

nodes.name: Pourbaix (E–pH) Diagram Construction
nodes.description: A Pourbaix diagram maps the thermodynamically stable species of an element in water as a function of potential and pH: redox-only equilibria give horizontal lines, acid-base equilibria vertical lines, and combined proton-electron equilibria sloped Nernst lines.

nodes.name: Spectroelectrochemistry
nodes.description: Spectroelectrochemistry couples potential control with in-situ spectroscopy: stepping the potential in an optically transparent cell generates redox species whose UV-Vis, IR or EPR signatures are recorded simultaneously to correlate spectra with oxidation states and intermediates.

nodes.name: Carbonate System & Alkalinity
nodes.description: Natural water chemistry is governed by the carbonate system: dissolved CO2 forms carbonic acid that dissociates stepwise to bicarbonate and carbonate, setting speciation versus pH; treating the system as open (fixed pCO2) or closed (fixed total carbon) and applying mass and charge balance yields the equilibrium pH and alkalinity.

nodes.name: Metal Oxidation (Ellingham)
nodes.description: Metal oxidation is analyzed with an Ellingham diagram of the oxidation free energy versus temperature: a negative free energy means the oxide is favored, and a reductant whose oxidation line lies below the metal's allows the oxide to be reduced (e.g. carbothermic reduction).

nodes.name: Arrhenius Plot for Activation Energy
nodes.description: Extract the activation energy and pre-exponential factor by measuring rate constants at several temperatures, linearizing the Arrhenius equation as ln k versus 1/T, and reading the activation energy from the slope and the pre-factor from the intercept.

nodes.name: Pressure Effects on Gas Reactions
nodes.description: Raising pressure increases gas-phase partial pressures and collision frequency, accelerating bimolecular reactions; unimolecular reactions show Lindemann fall-off where the apparent order changes with pressure, and changes in gas moles shift equilibrium.

nodes.name: Controlled Radical Polymerization (ATRP/RAFT)
nodes.description: Controlled/living radical polymerization keeps a low instantaneous radical concentration through a fast dynamic equilibrium between active and dormant chains; when the equilibrium strongly favors the dormant species, all chains grow at the same slow rate, giving predictable molar mass and low dispersity once the target is reached.

nodes.name: Phase Diagram (Gibbs Phase Rule)
nodes.description: A phase diagram is constructed from the Gibbs phase rule F = C - P + 2, which fixes the degrees of freedom: single-phase regions and the boundaries between them are mapped on temperature-composition axes, the lever rule gives phase amounts inside two-phase regions, and invariant points complete the diagram.

nodes.name: Entropic Rubber Elasticity
nodes.description: In a cross-linked elastomer the restoring force is entropic: stretching extends network chains and lowers their conformational entropy, so the chains recoil when released, a behavior captured by the statistical theory of rubber elasticity.

nodes.name: Glass Transition in Polymers
nodes.description: On cooling an amorphous polymer, free volume contracts until, below the glass transition temperature, segmental motion freezes and the material becomes a rigid glass; cooling rate and plasticizer content shift the observed Tg.

nodes.name: Flory–Huggins Solution Theory
nodes.description: Flory–Huggins lattice theory combines a small combinatorial entropy of mixing for long chains with an enthalpic χ interaction parameter; the resulting free energy predicts miscibility or phase separation of polymer solutions.

nodes.name: Kohn–Sham DFT Workflow
nodes.description: Kohn–Sham DFT recasts the interacting electron problem as non-interacting electrons in an effective potential: after choosing an exchange-correlation functional and basis, the density is iterated through the KS equations until self-consistency, then properties are computed.

nodes.name: Chemical Potential & Phase Equilibrium
nodes.description: Express each species' chemical potential as a function of activity, then drive multiphase systems toward equilibrium by transferring matter down the chemical-potential gradient until the potential of every component is equal in all phases.

nodes.name: Entropy Change & the Second Law
nodes.description: Compute the entropy change of a process via a reversible path, add the surroundings' entropy change, and use the sign of the total entropy of the universe to judge whether the process is spontaneous, reversible, or impossible.

nodes.name: First-Order Phase Transition (Clapeyron)
nodes.description: Two phases reach coexistence when their chemical potentials are equal; the Clapeyron equation then relates the slope of the phase boundary to the entropy and volume changes, and crossing the boundary drives the transition with its latent heat.

nodes.name: Ideal vs Real Solution Behavior
nodes.description: Model a mixture starting from ideal mixing, test for Raoult's-law behavior, and when deviations occur describe them with activity coefficients and an excess Gibbs energy to predict vapor pressures and phase equilibria.

nodes.name: Measurement and Analysis
nodes.description: Analytical chemistry, chromatography, spectroscopy, calibration, and validation.

nodes.name: Calibration Curve Construction
nodes.description: A calibration curve relates instrument signal to concentration: measure a standard series, regress signal on concentration, reject outliers or re-prepare if the fit is poor, confirm the linear dynamic range, and use the fitted equation to quantify unknowns.

nodes.name: Quantitative Chromatography (Internal Standard)
nodes.description: Quantitative chromatography spikes a sample with an internal standard, separates and detects the analytes, confirms baseline resolution, integrates peak areas, applies response factors relative to the internal standard, and converts to concentration via calibration.

nodes.name: Method Validation (QC)
nodes.description: Validating an analytical method demonstrates fitness for purpose by establishing specificity, linearity and range, accuracy and precision, and detection/quantitation limits; failing criteria trigger method adjustment, and robustness plus system suitability finalize validation.

nodes.name: Sample Preparation Workflow
nodes.description: Sample preparation turns a raw sample into an analysis-ready solution: take a representative subsample, digest solids or extract/clean up liquids, remove matrix interferences, adjust to the working concentration range, then preserve and label the aliquot.

nodes.name: Beer–Lambert Quantitation
nodes.description: Quantitative spectrophotometry chooses an analytical wavelength, builds a calibration from standards, verifies Beer's-law linearity, fits a calibration line, then measures the sample absorbance and interpolates its concentration.

nodes.name: Molecular Docking (Virtual Screening)
nodes.description: Molecular docking predicts how ligands bind a target: the receptor is prepared, ligand poses are generated and placed in the binding site, scored for affinity, resampled when poor, ranked, and iterated over a library to shortlist virtual-screening hits.

nodes.name: Basis Set & Method Selection
nodes.description: Choosing a computational protocol balances accuracy against cost: pick a method family (DFT vs correlated wavefunction), select a basis set with appropriate polarization/diffuse functions for the chemistry, and benchmark against reference data before committing.

nodes.name: Geometry Optimization
nodes.description: Geometry optimization finds a stationary point by iteratively stepping along energy gradients until they vanish; a subsequent frequency calculation confirms a true minimum (no imaginary modes) or signals a saddle that must be re-optimized.

nodes.name: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy
nodes.description: EIS applies a small AC perturbation across a range of frequencies, measures the complex impedance, and fits Nyquist/Bode data to an equivalent circuit to extract charge-transfer resistance, double-layer capacitance and diffusional (Warburg) behavior.

nodes.name: Fragment-Based Drug Discovery
nodes.description: Fragment-based discovery screens a library of small fragments by biophysical methods, confirms weak binders and their binding site by structure, then grows or links fragments through iterative medicinal chemistry to build a high-affinity lead.

nodes.name: Radiocarbon Dating
nodes.description: Radiocarbon dating exploits the fixed atmospheric 14C/12C ratio in living tissue: when an organism dies, uptake stops and 14C decays with a 5730-year half-life, so measuring the residual 14C and calibrating against tree-ring records yields a calendar age.

nodes.name: Nuclear Fusion (D–T)
nodes.description: Fusion joins light nuclei into a heavier one: only when enough energy is supplied to tunnel through the Coulomb barrier do deuterium and tritium fuse, releasing energy from the mass defect, and net power requires meeting the Lawson confinement criterion.

nodes.name: R/S Stereochemistry Assignment
nodes.description: Assign absolute configuration with the Cahn-Ingold-Prelog rules: locate each stereocenter, rank its four substituents by priority, orient the lowest priority away, and read 1->2->3 as clockwise (R) or counterclockwise (S), iterating over all centers.

nodes.name: Beer-Lambert Quantitative Analysis
nodes.description: Quantitative absorption spectroscopy relies on the Beer-Lambert law A = epsilon*b*c: a wavelength near the absorption maximum is selected, absorbance is kept within the linear range by dilution, a calibration curve is built from standards, and the unknown concentration is interpolated from its measured absorbance.

nodes.name: Harmonic Vibrational (IR) Frequencies
nodes.description: Vibrational frequencies follow from the Hessian at an optimized geometry: mass-weighting and diagonalization give normal modes; the absence of imaginary frequencies confirms a minimum, IR intensities come from dipole derivatives, and scaled frequencies simulate the IR spectrum.

nodes.name: Chromatographic Separation
nodes.description: Chromatography separates a mixture by differential partitioning between a mobile and a stationary phase: components with stronger stationary-phase affinity are retained longer; band broadening (van Deemter) limits resolution, which is tuned by adjusting phase and flow.

nodes.name: FT-IR Spectroscopy
nodes.description: FT-IR passes broadband infrared light through an interferometer and sample; bonds whose dipole moment changes absorb at characteristic frequencies, the interferogram is Fourier-transformed to a spectrum, and functional-group and fingerprint regions identify the compound.

nodes.name: Mass Spectrometry
nodes.description: Mass spectrometry ionizes a sample to molecular and fragment ions, accelerates them into a mass analyzer that separates by mass-to-charge ratio, and detects abundances; identifying the molecular ion and interpreting fragmentation reveals mass and structure.

nodes.name: Raman Spectroscopy
nodes.description: Raman spectroscopy detects inelastically scattered laser light: modes that change polarizability produce Stokes/anti-Stokes shifts after the intense Rayleigh line is filtered, giving a vibrational fingerprint that complements IR through the rule of mutual exclusion.

nodes.name: UV-Vis Spectroscopy
nodes.description: UV-Vis measures electronic absorption: light is scanned through a sample, chromophores promote electrons (pi->pi*, n->pi*), the absorbance is computed from transmitted intensity, and within the linear range Beer's law relates absorbance to concentration.

nodes.name: X-ray Diffraction Analysis
nodes.description: X-ray diffraction probes crystal structure: X-rays scatter off lattice planes and interfere constructively only when Bragg's law is satisfied, producing peaks versus 2-theta that are indexed and refined (e.g. Rietveld) to determine the unit cell and phase.

nodes.name: BET Surface Area Analysis
nodes.description: BET analysis measures specific surface area from multilayer gas physisorption: after degassing, nitrogen is dosed at increasing relative pressures, the BET equation is fit over its linear region to extract monolayer capacity, and the surface area follows from molecular cross-section.

nodes.name: Materials and Surfaces
nodes.description: Materials synthesis, surfaces, nanomaterials, deposition, adsorption, and defects.

nodes.name: Electrodeposition: Nucleation & Growth
nodes.description: At an applied overpotential, metal ions are reduced to adatoms that diffuse on the surface; only clusters exceeding a critical size survive to grow, following instantaneous or progressive nucleation before coalescing into a continuous film.

nodes.name: Coagulation-Flocculation Treatment
nodes.description: Coagulation-flocculation clarifies turbid water: a metal-salt coagulant is rapidly mixed to neutralize colloid surface charge and destabilize particles, then slow mixing promotes collisions that grow flocs; if flocs are large enough they settle out, otherwise aids are added and mixing continues before sedimentation yields clarified water.

nodes.name: Crystal Field Theory
nodes.description: Crystal field theory models ligands as point charges that split a metal's d-orbitals according to coordination geometry; comparing the splitting to the electron pairing energy predicts high- or low-spin configurations, color, and magnetic properties.

nodes.name: Composite Fabrication
nodes.description: Composite fabrication combines a matrix with a reinforcing phase: after selecting matrix chemistry, fibers or particles are surface-treated for wetting, then impregnated or infiltrated by the matrix, and interfacial bonding is verified before consolidation and curing produce the finished part.

nodes.name: Nanoparticle Synthesis (LaMer)
nodes.description: The LaMer model explains monodisperse nanoparticle synthesis: precursor decomposition raises monomer concentration until a critical supersaturation triggers a single burst of nucleation, which drops the concentration below threshold so no new nuclei form, and the existing nuclei grow by diffusion under capping-ligand control into uniform particles.

nodes.name: Emulsion Polymerization
nodes.description: Emulsion polymerization builds polymer inside surfactant micelles dispersed in water: above the critical micelle concentration a water-soluble initiator generates radicals that enter micelles to nucleate particles, monomer diffuses from droplets to feed propagation, and growth continues until droplets are exhausted, yielding a polymer latex.

nodes.name: Composite Stiffness (Rule of Mixtures)
nodes.description: The rule of mixtures predicts the elastic stiffness of a continuous-fiber composite from constituent properties and volume fractions: loading parallel to the fibers obeys the iso-strain Voigt bound while transverse loading obeys the iso-stress Reuss bound, with corrections for fiber orientation giving the effective modulus.

nodes.name: Crystal Structure & Unit Cells
nodes.description: Determining a crystal structure starts from the lattice-and-basis description: a unit cell and its Bravais lattice are identified, close-packed arrangements are classified as FCC/HCP versus BCC, coordination number and atomic packing factor are computed, and Miller indices label planes for relating structure to properties.

nodes.name: Doping & Point Defects
nodes.description: Doping a semiconductor introduces substitutional impurities that act as donors or acceptors: thermal ionization releases electrons or holes, charge neutrality is maintained through compensating defects described by Kroger-Vink notation, and the resulting carrier concentration sets the conductivity and type of the doped material.

nodes.name: Quantum Confinement in Nanocrystals
nodes.description: When a semiconductor crystal shrinks below the exciton Bohr radius, charge carriers become spatially confined, their energy levels become discrete, and the band gap widens with decreasing size; this size-tunable gap shifts the absorption and emission, allowing the synthesis size to be adjusted to hit a target wavelength.

nodes.name: Polymorphism & Phase Transformation
nodes.description: Polymorphic systems can crystallize into several structures: kinetics often favor a metastable form first (Ostwald's rule of stages) while thermodynamics favor the most stable polymorph, and applying temperature, pressure, or a seed crosses the transformation barrier to convert toward the controlled target polymorph.

nodes.name: Physisorption vs Chemisorption
nodes.description: Adsorption on a surface is classified by the interaction: weak van der Waals attraction gives reversible physisorption, while electron sharing gives chemisorption with a chemical bond, which may be activated (with a barrier) or non-activated.

nodes.name: Surface Reconstruction & Relaxation
nodes.description: A freshly created surface has high-energy dangling bonds; to lower its energy the top layers relax (change spacing) or reconstruct (rebond into a new periodicity), behavior that adsorbates can further alter and that is probed by LEED or STM.

nodes.name: Semiconductor Photocatalysis (TiO2)
nodes.description: In TiO2 photocatalysis, photons above the band gap excite electron-hole pairs; charges either recombine (losing energy) or migrate to the surface, where they drive redox reactions that generate reactive oxygen species and degrade adsorbed pollutants.

nodes.name: Wetting & Contact Angle
nodes.description: Wetting is governed by the balance of solid-vapor, solid-liquid and liquid-vapor interfacial tensions through Young's equation; the resulting contact angle classifies wetting, complete spreading or non-wetting, and roughness modifies it via the Wenzel/Cassie-Baxter regimes.

nodes.name: Supported Metal Catalyst Preparation
nodes.description: Supported metal catalysts are made by impregnating a high-area oxide support with a metal precursor, drying, calcining to the oxide, and reducing to metal nanoparticles; dispersion is measured by chemisorption and tuned by adjusting loading and conditions.

nodes.name: Computational and Quantum Chemistry
nodes.description: Quantum calculations, DFT, molecular dynamics, orbitals, and reaction pathways.

nodes.name: Ab Initio Hartree–Fock SCF
nodes.description: Hartree–Fock solves the electronic structure self-consistently: from a geometry and basis set, integrals are computed, an initial guess builds the Fock matrix, the Roothaan equations are solved for new orbitals and density, and the cycle repeats until the energy converges.

nodes.name: Electronic Excited States (TD-DFT)
nodes.description: Excited states are computed from the ground state with time-dependent DFT: solving the Casida equations yields excitation energies and oscillator strengths, selection rules classify transitions as bright or dark, and Franck–Condon factors shape the simulated UV-Vis spectrum.

nodes.name: Molecular Dynamics Setup
nodes.description: Setting up an MD simulation: define the system and force field, build and minimize the configuration, assign velocities and equilibrate under a thermostat/barostat, then run production dynamics with a stable integration timestep before analyzing the trajectory.

nodes.name: Molecular Orbitals via LCAO
nodes.description: Molecular orbitals are built from atomic orbitals: only AOs of matching symmetry and similar energy combine, generating bonding and antibonding MOs that are ordered by energy and filled by the Aufbau principle to give bond order and an MO diagram.

nodes.name: Lithium-Ion Battery Operation
nodes.description: A lithium-ion cell shuttles Li+ between a graphite anode and a layered-oxide cathode: during discharge lithium leaves the anode and intercalates into the cathode while electrons power the load, and charging reverses the flow, with SEI growth driving aging.

nodes.name: Electroplating
nodes.description: Electroplating reduces metal cations from a bath onto a cathodic workpiece; adatoms diffuse, nucleate and grow into a coating, while a sacrificial anode of the plating metal dissolves to replenish the bath and additives control deposit uniformity.

nodes.name: PEM Fuel Cell Operation
nodes.description: A proton-exchange-membrane fuel cell oxidizes hydrogen at the anode, conducts protons through the membrane while electrons do work in the external circuit, and reduces oxygen at the cathode; the sluggish oxygen reduction is accelerated by a Pt catalyst.

nodes.name: Collision Theory & Temperature
nodes.description: Collision theory explains temperature dependence: only collisions with energy above the activation energy and correct orientation react, and raising temperature broadens the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution so a larger fraction of collisions is effective.

nodes.name: Tropospheric Ozone (Photochemical Smog)

nodes.name: Dose–Response & EC50
nodes.description: Pharmacodynamic characterization: apply a range of doses, measure the response, fit a sigmoidal concentration-response (Hill) curve to extract potency (EC50/IC50) and efficacy, and assess cooperativity and the therapeutic window.

nodes.name: Structure–Activity Relationship Cycle
nodes.description: An SAR optimization cycle: hypothesize how structure affects activity, design and synthesize analogues, assay potency and selectivity, update the SAR model when activity is not yet optimal, and converge on a pharmacophore and optimized candidate.

nodes.name: Hit-to-Lead ADMET Optimization
nodes.description: Hit-to-lead progression: confirm and triage hits, assess potency, profile ADMET, and iteratively modify structure to satisfy drug-likeness, improve solubility and metabolic stability, and clear toxicity flags before nominating a lead series.

nodes.name: Nuclear Chemistry
nodes.description: Existing collection from metadata.

nodes.name: Radioactive Decay Kinetics
nodes.description: An unstable nuclide decays by a mode set by its neutron-to-proton ratio (beta-minus, beta-plus/electron capture, or alpha), emitting particles and energy; decay follows first-order kinetics with a characteristic half-life, sometimes through a chain to a stable nuclide.

nodes.name: Radioisotope Production (Neutron Activation)
nodes.description: Neutron activation produces radioisotopes by irradiating a stable target so it captures neutrons via (n,γ) to form a radioactive nuclide; after sufficient activity is reached, the product is chemically separated, purified and used as a tracer or radiopharmaceutical.

nodes.name: Polymer Chemistry
nodes.description: Existing collection from metadata.

nodes.name: Free-Radical Chain Polymerization
nodes.description: Free-radical chain polymerization proceeds by initiator decomposition, repeated monomer addition during propagation, and chain-end loss by combination/disproportionation or chain transfer, governing molecular weight and conversion.

nodes.name: Glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas)
nodes.description: Glycolysis converts glucose to two pyruvate through an energy-investment phase that spends ATP to make fructose-1,6-bisphosphate, cleavage to two triose phosphates, and a payoff phase that oxidizes glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate to generate NADH and substrate-level ATP, requiring NAD+ regeneration to continue.
